observations: The trigger's icon (ct-icon, defaulting to information-mark) is always rendered aria-hidden — it is decorative, matching CivicTheme's own icon component. The button's accessible name comes entirely from the `label` attribute (rendered as both aria-label and title). `label` is a required field in TooltipSchema, but the Lit component itself does not enforce a non-empty value at runtime; an implementer who omits it ships an icon-only button with no accessible name at all.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: The trigger-to-popup relationship is established programmatically by the component itself, not left to the implementer: the popup gets role="tooltip" and an id, and the trigger gets aria-describedby pointing at that id whenever the popup is open (via the Zag.js tooltip machine's connect() output). No manual id/aria-describedby wiring is required.
The component is a disclosure trigger, not a form input; it collects no user data.
The popup's presence and its dismiss control are conveyed through position, an icon, and a visually-hidden accessible name — not through color alone.

Both themes were verified against civictheme.variables.css-sourced tokens: light theme is #fafbfb text on a #00698f popup background, dark theme is #003a4f text on a #61daff popup background — both comfortably exceed 4.5:1.
Popup text uses rem-based typography tokens (--ct-typography-label-extra-small-*) and remains readable at 200% zoom; the popup itself does not clip or truncate text at larger sizes.
Popup content is a plain string rendered as real text, not an image.

The popup has a fixed width (--ct-tooltip-width / --ct-tooltip-width-mobile) rather than reflowing with the viewport; compliance for very long `content` strings depends on the implementer keeping the text reasonably short.

Composing ct-button's tertiary variant unmodified for the dismiss control would render its icon in --ct-color-interaction-{light,dark}-background — the exact same color as the popup's own background (--ct-tooltip-*-description-background-color is an alias of that same interaction-background token), making the close icon invisible (verified by screenshot before the fix). Fixed by scoping an override of that custom property to the dismiss control's wrapper so its effective color resolves to the popup's own (guaranteed-contrasting) text color, without modifying ct-button itself.
Popup text remains readable when line/letter/word spacing is increased per the criterion's multipliers; no fixed-height container clips the text.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)
observations: This is the success criterion the component exists to satisfy, and the Zag.js tooltip machine (not manual implementation) provides all three sub-requirements: Dismissible — closeOnEscape defaults to true, so Escape closes the popup without moving focus; Hoverable — `interactive: true` is set so moving the pointer from the trigger onto the popup (e.g. to reach the dismiss control) keeps it open instead of dismissing it; Persistent — the popup has no arbitrary auto-hide timer, only a short closeDelay after the pointer/focus actually leaves, so it remains visible for as long as it is relevant.
The trigger is a real <button>, reachable and operable via Tab/Enter/Space; focusing it opens the popup, and the dismiss control inside is itself a focusable, real ct-button.
Focus moves through the trigger and (when open) the dismiss control using standard Tab order; nothing traps focus inside the popup.
No single-character key shortcuts are implemented.
The openDelay/closeDelay are short UI debounces (hundreds of milliseconds), not a time limit on completing a task.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: Focus order follows DOM order (trigger, then dismiss control when open); the component does not manipulate tabindex.
The trigger is a button, not a link.
Compliance depends on the implementer providing a `label` (and `content`) that clearly describes what the popup will disclose, since both are implementer-supplied strings.

The trigger has an explicit :focus-visible outline in both themes (--ct-outline-width/offset with --ct-outline-light/dark), matching the token values used by other composed components (e.g. ct-button).
The popup is positioned by the floating-ui-based Zag machine with a `flip` fallback so it does not render on top of the element that triggered it.
Only simple pointer hover/click is used; no multipoint or path-based gesture.

Opening/closing fire on pointerenter/pointerleave/focus/blur/click completion, not on an unabortable pointerdown; closeOnPointerDown is Zag's default and can be disabled if a down-event action is ever added.

The trigger and dismiss control are icon-only (no visible text label to match against); both get their accessible name from aria-label/visually-hidden text rather than visible text, so this criterion — which concerns visible text that is part of the accessible name — does not apply.

The trigger's hit area is only as large as the rendered icon (no padding is added around it in any `icon-size`); compliance for the 24x24px minimum depends on the implementer choosing a large enough `icon-size`, or CivicTheme's general exception for inline text-adjacent controls applying.
The document's lang attribute is outside this component's scope.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting a lang attribute on `content`/`label` if their language differs from the surrounding page.

Focusing the trigger reveals the popup, but WCAG's own definition of "change of context" explicitly excludes content like a tooltip appearing as a result of focus — it is supplementary information, not a change of context (new window, different content replacing the current view, etc.), so this criterion is met by design.
The component has no input controls.
Compliance depends on the implementer using the same `icon` (default information-mark) and `label` wording for tooltips serving the same purpose across a site.
The component has no form-validation role.
The component is not a form input.

The Zag.js machine provides role/state wiring automatically (role="tooltip" on the popup, aria-describedby on the trigger when open, data-state reflecting open/closed) — but the trigger's own accessible Name still depends on the implementer supplying a non-empty `label`, same caveat as 1.1.1.
The popup opening is a direct result of the user's own hover/focus/click action on a visible control, not an out-of-band status update requiring an aria-live region.
